baseline_dominance.justification: Construction removes the AS from the per-scope authorization path entirely — agent generates proofs locally post-enrollment. OAuth/OIDC structurally requires AS participation at token issuance, so the AS always sees the (agent, RS) pair. WIMSE/SD-JWT hides attributes from verifiers, not issuers. spiffe_engineer Attacks 1-2 confirm the construction survives against SPIFFE and WIMSE baselines on this specific property. rfc7662_advocate Attack 3 correctly notes Threat B (RS-to-RS correlation) is already solved by PPIDs, but Threat A (AS-side correlation) is the exclusive and provably unachievable-by-baseline differentiator.

formal_security_argument.justification: Threat model is explicit (adversary controls AS, subset of RSes, network metadata). Named assumptions (Poseidon-PRF, PLONK knowledge soundness, Poseidon collision resistance, DLP on Baby Jubjub) are stated. Hybrid reduction sketch is provided. However, the cryptographer's Attack 1 is fatal to the game definition: scopeId is a public input (signal index 5), so the IND-UNL-AS game is trivially won with advantage 1 by reading public signals. The reduction's Hybrid 3 claim that 'public inputs are the same in both orderings' is false — π_left embeds scopeId_b and π_right embeds scopeId_{1-b}. The intended property (agent-linkage across scopes) may hold, but the formal game as written does not capture it. Reduction is broken against the stated game.

implementability.justification: Every circuit element maps to existing Bolyra primitives: Poseidon2/5, BinaryMerkleRoot(20), PLONK proving system, LessThan(64), Num2Bits. No new primitives introduced. Constraint breakdown is detailed (~15,600 total), proving time target (<3s PLONK) is within Phase 1 budget and comparable to existing AgentPolicy circuit (~16,000 constraints, <5s target). Verification is standard single PLONK check. The primitive mapping table in §5 is comprehensive with spec references.

adversarial_survival.justification: Multiple Tier 3 attacks surface real weaknesses: (1) cryptographer Attack 1 — game definition is trivially broken via public scopeId, requiring reformulation; (2) cryptographer Attack 2 — agentMerkleRoot is a cross-scope fingerprint reducing anonymity set, potentially to 1 in sparse deployments; (3) cu_ciso Attack 1 — audit trail paradox where GLBA Reg P motivation conflicts with NCUA Part 748 audit obligations; (4) cu_ciso Attack 4 — revocation mechanism is referenced but never specified; (5) rfc7662_advocate Attack 4 — blindingNonce freshness is unverifiable by RS. The underlying PRF-based unlinkability argument survives conceptually (the crypto idea is sound for agent-linkage), but the formal claim as stated is broken and multiple operational gaps weaken deployment viability. Scoring 1: real attacks that weaken but do not fundamentally destroy the core cryptographic insight.

scenario_fit.justification: SECU (North Carolina) is named as a concrete stakeholder with specific regulatory requirement (GLBA Reg P — AS must not learn member merchant graph). The property is load-bearing: without AS-exclusion from per-scope authorization, SECU-as-AS can trivially reconstruct member financial activity. Healthcare variant (Kaiser Permanente) provides a second concrete scenario. The cu_ciso audit trail paradox is a real gap but concerns reconciling the privacy property with competing regulations, not the scenario's validity — the need for the property is genuine and regulatory.

gaps: Reformulate IND-UNL-AS game: current game is trivially won because scopeId is a public signal. The correct game should challenge A to distinguish same-agent vs different-agent proofs at known scopes, not which-proof-covers-which-scope.
Address Merkle root epoch anonymity set attack: specify minimum batch enrollment size or root update policy so agentMerkleRoot does not reduce anonymity set to 1 in sparse deployments.

Specify revocation mechanism: §2 step 5c references scopeNullifier revocation but no registry design exists. Need a revocation scheme that does not create a linkage oracle, with propagation latency SLA.
Add agentSecret rotation protocol: current design has no forward secrecy, no key lifecycle, no compromise recovery. Specify expiryTimestamp upper bound and re-enrollment procedure.
Reconcile GLBA Reg P privacy claim with NCUA Part 748 / GLBA Safeguards audit obligations: design a privacy-preserving audit mechanism (e.g., encrypted per-scope logs openable under dual control).
Specify blindingNonce generation requirements: minimum entropy, CSPRNG mandate, or RS-issued nonce challenge for liveness — current design makes freshness unverifiable by RS.

Address TLS-terminating proxy threat: enterprise deployments route through AS-controlled infrastructure that can read scopeId in cleartext. Either encrypt public signals to RS public key or bind RS identity into proof.
